void add_eas_records(const std::string& table_name,

}

<answer>```cpp
// add+modify_eas_records.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <map>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>

#include "eas_common.hpp"
using namespace sqlgen;
using namespace sqlgen::literals;

// Helper function to convert a value to SQL binding
template<typename T>
void bind_value(sqlite3_stmt* stmt, int index, const T& value) {
  if constexpr (std::is_same_v<T, std::string>) {
    sqlite3_bind_text(stmt, index, value.c_str(), -1, SQLITE_TRANSIENT);
  } else if constexpr (std::is_integral_v<T>) {
    sqlite3_bind_int(stmt, index, value);
  } else if constexpr (std::is_floating_point_v<T>) {
    sqlite3_bind_double(stmt, index, value);
  } else {
    static_assert(!std::is_same_v<T, T>, "Unsupported type for SQL binding");
  }
}

template<typename T>
void add_eas_records(const std::string& table_name,
                     const std::vector<T>& records) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  
  // Get field information using reflection
  auto field_info = rfl::reflect_type<T>();
  const auto& field_names = field_info.names();
  
  // Build SQL statement
  std::string sql = "INSERT INTO " + table_name + " (";
  for (size_t i = 0; i < field_names.size(); ++i) {
    if (i > 0) sql += ", ";
    sql += field_names[i];
  }
  sql += ") VALUES (";
  for (size_t i = 0; i < field_names.size(); ++i) {
    if (i > 0) sql += ", ";
    sql += "?";
  }
  sql += ")";
  
  auto stmt = conn.prepare(sql).value();
  
  for (const auto& record : records) {
    auto values = rfl::to_vector(record);
    
    // Handle optional values
    for (auto& val : values) {
      if (!val.has_value()) {
        val = std::nullopt;
      }
    }
    
    // Bind values to prepared statement
    for (size_t i = 0; i < values.size(); ++i) {
      const auto& field = field_info.fields()[i];
      
      if (field.type().name() == "optional") {
        if (values[i].has_value()) {
          using ValueType = std::decay_t<decltype(values[i].value())>;
          bind_value(stmt.get(), i + 1, values[i].value());
        } else {
          sqlite3_bind_null(stmt.get(), i + 1);
        }
      } else {
        using ValueType = std::decay_t<decltype(values[i])>;
        bind_value(stmt.get(), i + 1, values[i]);
      }
    }
    
    stmt.execute().value();
  }
}

void modify_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const std::string& record_id,
                              const std::string& col_name,
                              const std::string& new_value) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  
  // Map table names to primary key information
  static const std::map<std::string, std::pair<std::string, std::string>> 
      primary_keys = {
          {"Student", {"SNo", "TEXT"}},
          {"Course", {"CID", "INTEGER"}},
          {"Score", {"ScID", "INTEGER"}},
          {"Major", {"MID", "INTEGER"}}
      };
  
  auto it = primary_keys.find(table_name);
  if (it == primary_keys.end()) {
    throw std::invalid_argument("Unknown table: " + table_name);
  }
  
  const std::string& pk_col = it->second.first;
  const std::string& pk_type = it->second.second;
  
  std::string sql = "UPDATE " + table_name + " SET " + col_name + " = ? WHERE " + pk_col + " = ?";
  auto stmt = conn.prepare(sql).value();
  
  // Bind new value (assuming it's a string for simplicity)
  stmt.bind(1, new_value);
  
  // Bind record_id with appropriate type conversion
  if (pk_type == "INTEGER") {
    try {
      int id_val = std::stoi(record_id);
      stmt.bind(2, id_val);
    } catch (const std::exception&) {
      throw std::invalid_argument("Invalid integer record_id: " + record_id);
    }
  } else {
    stmt.bind(2, record_id);
  }
  
  stmt.execute().value();
}

void delete_eas_records_by_id(const std::string& table_name,
                              const std::string& record_id) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path).value();
  
  static const std::map<std::string, std::pair<std::string, std::string>> 
      primary_keys = {
          {"Student", {"SNo", "TEXT"}},
          {"Course", {"CID", "INTEGER"}},
          {"Score", {"ScID", "INTEGER"}},
          {"Major", {"MID", "INTEGER"}}
      };
  
  auto it = primary_keys.find(table_name);
  if (it == primary_keys.end()) {
    throw std::invalid_argument("Unknown table: " + table_name);
  }
  
  const std::string& pk_col = it->second.first;
  const std::string& pk_type = it->second.second;
  
  std::string sql = "DELETE FROM " + table_name + " WHERE " + pk_col + " = ?";
  auto stmt = conn.prepare(sql).value();
  
  if (pk_type == "INTEGER") {
    try {
      int id_val = std::stoi(record_id);
      stmt.bind(1, id_val);
    } catch (const std::exception&) {
      throw std::invalid_argument("Invalid integer record_id: " + record_id);
    }
  } else {
    stmt.bind(1, record_id);
  }
  
  stmt.execute().value();
}
```

This solution provides:
1. `add_eas_records`: Uses reflection to dynamically generate INSERT statements based on the provided table name and records
2. `modify_eas_records_by_id`: Handles different primary key types and constructs parameterized UPDATE statements
3. `delete_eas_records_by_id`: Similar to modify but for DELETE operations
4. Proper handling of optional values and type conversions
5. Error handling for invalid inputs

The solution leverages the sqlgen library's capabilities while providing a flexible interface for database operations.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace delete_inactive_users {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> last_login;
  std::string status = "active";
};

int delete_inactive_users() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Start transaction
    sqlgen::Transaction transaction(conn);
    
    // Build and execute DELETE query
    auto query = sqlgen::delete_from<User> | where("last_login" < sqlgen::DateTime("2025-01-01"));
    conn.execute(query);
    
    // Commit transaction
    auto commit_result = transaction.commit();
    return commit_result ? 0 : 1;
  }
  catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // In case of exception, transaction is automatically rolled back
    return 1;
  }
}

} // namespace delete_inactive_users
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Transaction Safety**: A transaction is started using `sqlgen::Transaction` to ensure atomicity.

3. **Query Construction**: The DELETE query is built using `sqlgen::delete_from<User>` with a WHERE clause filtering users who last logged in before January 1, 2025.

4. **Execution**: The query is executed within the transaction scope.

5. **Commit Handling**: The transaction is committed, and the result (success/failure) is returned as an integer.

6. **Error Handling**: Any exceptions during execution trigger automatic rollback, and the function returns `1` to indicate failure.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <optional>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace test_design_eas_database {

  struct Student {
    rfl::Field<"SNo", std::string> SNo;
    rfl::Field<"SName", std::string> SName;
    rfl::Field<"Sex", std::optional<int>> Sex = 1;
    rfl::Field<"Birthday", std::optional<std::string>> Birthday;
    rfl::Field<"Major", std::optional<int>> Major;
    rfl::Field<"Scorses", std::optional<float>> Scorses = 0.0f;
    rfl::Field<"Remark", std::optional<std::string>> Remark;
  };

  struct Course {
    rfl::Field<"CID", int> CID;
    rfl::Field<"CName", std::string> CName;
    rfl::Field<"Term", std::optional<int>> Term = 1;
    rfl::Field<"CTime", std::optional<int>> CTime;
    rfl::Field<"Credit", float> Credit = 0.0f;
  };

  struct Score {
    rfl::Field<"SNo", std::string> SNo;
    rfl::Field<"CID", int> CID;
    rfl::Field<"Score", std::optional<float>> Score;
  };

  void design_eas_database() {
    const auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables with proper constraints
    sqlgen::create_table<Student>(conn);
    sqlgen::create_table<Course>(conn);
    sqlgen::create_table<Score>(conn);
  }

}
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Struct Definitions**: Each struct uses `rfl::Field` to explicitly define field names and types, ensuring proper mapping to SQL columns.
2. **Nullable Fields**: Uses `std::optional` for fields that allow NULL values in the database.
3. **Default Values**: Default values are set directly in the field declarations (e.g., `Sex = 1`, `Term = 1`, `Credit = 0.0f`, `Scorses = 0.0f`).
4. **Primary Keys**: 
   - `Student::SNo` is defined as `std::string` (non-optional) to enforce NOT NULL constraint and serve as PRIMARY KEY.
   - `Course::CID` is defined as `int` (non-optional) to serve as auto-incrementing PRIMARY KEY.
   - `Score` uses a composite primary key through its field ordering and non-optional types.
5. **Foreign Keys**: The field names (`SNo` and `CID`) in the `Score` struct naturally reference the primary keys of `Student` and `Course` respectively, which the `sqlgen` library should recognize and enforce.

This solution leverages the `rfl` reflection library to ensure type-safe database schema generation while maintaining the specified constraints and relationships.</answer>

  int user_id;

};

  std::string username;

    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    // Create tables

    conn.create_table<Post>();
    
    // Execute the query using raw SQL

        R"( INNER JOIN Post p ON u.user_id = p.user_id)"
        R"( WHERE p.created_at >= DATE('2025-12-01') AND p.created_at <= DATE('2025-12-31');)"
    );

        ActiveUser user;
        user.user_id = row.get<int>("user_id");
        user.username = row.get<std::string>("username");
        active_users.push_back(user);
    }
    
    return active_users;
}

}  // namespace query_active_users_posts

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace query_course_enrollment_stats {

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> course_id;
  std::string title;
};

struct Enrollment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> enrollment_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Course, "course_id"> course_id;
  std::optional<float> grade;
};

struct CourseStats {
  std::string title;
  int student_count;
  std::optional<double> avg_grade;
};

struct CourseStatsView {};

std::vector<CourseStats> query_course_enrollment_stats() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to SQLite database
  auto conn = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");

  // Create tables based on struct definitions
  conn.create_table<Course>();
  conn.create_table<Enrollment>();

  // Execute the enrollment statistics query
  auto result = conn.and_then(SQLGEN_LITERAL(
    "SELECT c.title, COUNT(e.enrollment_id) as student_count, AVG(e.grade) as avg_grade "
    "FROM Course c "
    "LEFT JOIN Enrollment e ON c.course_id = e.course_id "
    "GROUP BY c.course_id, c.title"
  )).transform([](auto&& rows) {
    std::vector<CourseStats> stats;
    for (const auto& row : rows) {
      CourseStats cs;
      cs.title = row.get<std::string>("title");
      cs.student_count = row.get<int>("student_count");
      
      // Handle average grade (NULL if no grades exist)
      if (row.has("avg_grade")) {
        cs.avg_grade = row.get<double>("avg_grade");
      } else {
        cs.avg_grade = std::nullopt;
      }
      
      stats.push_back(std::move(cs));
    }
    return stats;
  });

  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace query_course_enrollment_stats
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect()` to establish connection to "test_db.sqlite"

2. **Table Creation**: Automatically creates `Course` and `Enrollment` tables using `create_table<Struct>()` based on the struct definitions with proper constraints

3. **Query Execution**: Executes a SQL query that:
   - Performs a LEFT JOIN between Course and Enrollment tables
   - Groups results by course to calculate statistics per course
   - Handles NULL grades properly using `std::optional`

4. **Result Processing**: Maps the query results to `CourseStats` objects, properly handling:
   - Course titles
   - Student counts (including courses with zero enrollments)
   - Average grades (using `std::optional` to represent NULL values)

5. **Error Handling**: Uses `result.value()` to extract the final result, assuming successful execution as per the problem requirements

The solution leverages the `sqlgen` library's capabilities for database operations while maintaining type safety and proper handling of optional values.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_customer_purchase_history {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Customer, "customer_id"> customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  double total_amount;
};

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Order, "order_id"> order_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Product, "product_id"> product_id;
  int quantity;
};

struct PurchaseHistory {
  std::string product_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
  int quantity;
};

std::vector<PurchaseHistory> query_customer_purchase_history(int customer_id) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create tables if they don't exist
    conn.create_table<Customer>();
    conn.create_table<Order>();
    conn.create_table<OrderItem>();
    conn.create_table<Product>();
    
    // Execute the query
    auto result = conn.and_then([&](auto&& db) {
        return sqlgen::from<Customer>(db)
            .inner_join<Order>("customer_id")
            .inner_join<OrderItem>("order_id")
            .inner_join<Product>("product_id")
            .where("customer_id", customer_id)
            .select("name", "order_date", "quantity")
            .order_by("order_date", sqlgen::desc)
            .transform([](auto&& row) {
                return PurchaseHistory{
                    .product_name = row.get<std::string>("name"),
                    .order_date = row.get<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">>("order_date"),
                    .quantity = row.get<int>("quantity")
                };
            })
            .value();
    });
    
    return result;
}

}  // namespace query_customer_purchase_history
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database
2. Creates the required tables using the struct definitions
3. Executes a multi-table join query to retrieve purchase history
4. Maps the results to the `PurchaseHistory` struct
5. Returns the results ordered by most recent orders</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics {

struct PageVisit {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> visit_id;
  std::string url;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> visit_time;
};

struct DailyTraffic {
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

struct DailyTrafficView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "DailyTrafficView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string visit_date;
  int total_visits;
  int unique_pages;
};

/**
 * Function: Calculate daily traffic metrics from a log table.
 * 
 * @return List of daily traffic metrics
 */
std::vector<DailyTraffic> query_daily_traffic_metrics() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        return conn.read<std::vector<DailyTraffic>>(
            R"(SELECT strftime('%Y-%m-%d', visit_time) as visit_date, "
              "COUNT(*) as total_visits, "
              "COUNT(DISTINCT url) as unique_pages "
              "FROM PageVisit "
              "GROUP BY visit_date "
              "ORDER BY visit_date)"
        );
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace query_daily_traffic_metrics
```</answer>

First, the problem states that the Employee table has emp_id, name, and manager_id. The goal is to get each employee's name and their manager's name. Since some employees might not have a manager (like top-level executives), we need to use a LEFT JOIN to include those records where manager_id is NULL.

Looking at the provided code structure, there's a struct EmployeeManager that holds the employee name and an optional manager name. The function should return a vector of these structs.

The SQL query needed is a self-join where we join the Employee table with itself. The left table (e1) represents the employee, and the right table (e2) represents the manager. The join condition is e1.manager_id = e2.emp_id. Using LEFT JOIN ensures that even if an employee doesn't have a manager (manager_id is NULL), they still appear in the results with a NULL manager name.

Now, translating this into sqlgen syntax. The sqlgen library allows writing queries using C++ objects. So I need to create aliases for the two instances of the Employee table. Let's call them e1 and e2. Then perform the LEFT JOIN on e1.manager_id equals e2.emp_id.

In sqlgen, the query builder would look something like this:

auto query = sqlgen::read<std::vector<EmployeeManager>> | 
             from<Employee>(as("e1")) |
             left_join<Employee>(on("e1.manager_id", "e2.emp_id"), as("e2")) |
             select("e1.name", "e2.name");

Wait, but how exactly does sqlgen handle table aliases and column selections? Let me check the sqlgen documentation or examples. Oh right, sqlgen uses a fluent interface where you chain operations. The from clause takes the table type and an alias, then the left_join specifies the condition and the other table's alias. The select clause would map the columns to the result struct.

But the result struct EmployeeManager has fields 'employee' and 'manager'. So the SELECT clause should select e1.name as employee and e2.name as manager. In sqlgen, when you use the select method, you can specify the column names and their aliases which map to the struct fields.

#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>

#include <vector>

namespace query_employee_hierarchy {

struct Employee {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> emp_id;
  std::string name;
  std::optional<int> manager_id;
};

struct EmployeeManager {
  std::string employee;
  std::optional<std::string> manager;
};

std::vector<EmployeeManager> query_employee_hierarchy() {
    return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        | sqlgen::read<std::vector<EmployeeManager>>
        | from<Employee>("e1")
        | left_join<Employee>("e2", "e1.manager_id = e2.emp_id")
        | select("e1.name", "e2.name")
        .value();
}

}  // namespace query_employee_hierarchy
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **Query Construction**: 
   - `sqlgen::read<std::vector<EmployeeManager>>` specifies the expected return type
   - `from<Employee>("e1")` creates an alias "e1" for the Employee table
   - `left_join<Employee>("e2", "e1.manager_id = e2.emp_id")` performs a self-join with alias "e2" for manager records
   - `select("e1.name", "e2.name")` retrieves employee and manager names

3. **Result Handling**: The `.value()` extracts the result from the `std::optional` returned by the query execution.

4. **NULL Handling**: The `LEFT JOIN` ensures employees without managers (where `manager_id` is `NULL`) are included with an empty `manager` field in the result.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace query_high_value_orders {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  double total_amount;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct OrderView {
  static constexpr const char* tablename = "HighValueOrderView";
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string name;
  double total_amount;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

std::vector<OrderView> query_high_value_orders() {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  auto session = conn.session();
  
  return session.select_from<Order>()
    .inner_join<Customer>([](const auto& o, const auto& c) {
      return o.customer_id == c.customer_id;
    })
    .where([](const auto& o) { return o.total_amount > 1000; })
    .order_by([](const auto& o) { return o.total_amount; }, sqlgen::SortOrder::descending)
    .collect<std::vector<OrderView>>();

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace query_search_books_by_criteria {

struct Book {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> book_id;
  std::string title;
  std::string author;
  int year;
  std::string genre;
  double price;
};

struct BookFilters {
  std::optional<std::string> title_keyword;
  std::optional<int> min_year;
  std::optional<std::string> genre;
  std::optional<double> max_price;
};

std::vector<Book> query_search_books_by_criteria(const BookFilters& filters) {
    // Connect to SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Build the query dynamically
    auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Book>();
    
    // Add WHERE conditions based on provided filters
    if (filters.title_keyword) {
        query = query.where(sqlgen::like(Book::title, "%" + *filters.title_keyword + "%"));
    }
    
    if (filters.min_year) {
        query = query.where(Book::year >= *filters.min_year);
    }
    
    if (filters.genre) {
        query = query.where(Book::genre == *filters.genre);
    }
    
    if (filters.max_price) {
        query = query.where(Book::price <= *filters.max_price);
    }
    
    // Execute the query and retrieve results
    auto result = sqlgen::read<std::vector<Book>>(conn, query);
    
    return result;
}

}  // namespace query_search_books_by_criteria
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Dynamic Query Construction**: 
   - Starts with a base `SELECT * FROM Book` query
   - Conditionally adds `WHERE` clauses using `sqlgen::like()` for title searches and direct comparisons for year/price filters
   - Only includes filters that have non-null values

3. **Execution**: Uses `sqlgen::read()` to execute the query and convert results to a `std::vector<Book>`.

4. **Type Safety**: Leverages `sqlgen::PrimaryKey` and proper type mappings for robust database operations.

The solution handles all edge cases including when no filters are provided (returns all books) and when only specific filters are applied.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace schema_migration_add_column {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
};

struct UserAfter {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::string phone_number;
  bool is_active;
};

/**
 * Function: Perform schema migration to add new columns.
 * 
 * @return true if migration succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool migration_add_column() {
  try {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Execute ALTER TABLE statements to add new columns
    conn.execute("ALTER TABLE User ADD COLUMN phone_number VARCHAR(20);");
    conn.execute("ALTER TABLE User ADD COLUMN is_active INTEGER DEFAULT 1;");
    
    return true;
  } 
  catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Log the error (optional) and return false on failure
    // std::cerr << "Migration failed: " << e.what() << std::endl;
    return false;
  }
}

} // namespace schema_migration_add_column
```

1. **Database Connection:** Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **Schema Modification:** Executes two `ALTER TABLE` statements:

   - Adds `is_active` as INTEGER with default value 1 (representing true)
3. **Error Handling:** Wraps the database operations in a try-catch block to handle potential errors (e.g., column already exists)
4. **Return Value:** Returns `true` on successful migration, `false` if any operation fails

The solution leverages sqlgen's SQLite backend to perform the schema migration while maintaining proper error handling.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace soft_delete_records {

struct Document {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> doc_id;
  std::string title;
  int is_deleted = 0;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> deleted_at;
};

bool soft_delete_records(const std::vector<int>& ids) {
    if (ids.empty()) return true;
    
    try {
        auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
        sqlgen::begin_transaction(conn);
        
        // Generate placeholders for the IN clause
        std::string placeholders;
        for (size_t i = 0; i < ids.size(); ++i) {
            placeholders += "?";
            if (i < ids.size() - 1) {
                placeholders += ", ";
            }
        }
        
        std::string query = "UPDATE Document SET is_deleted = 1, deleted_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP WHERE doc_id IN (" + placeholders + ")";
        
        auto result = conn.execute(query, ids);
        
        if (!result) {
            sqlgen::rollback(conn);
            return false;
        }
        
        sqlgen::commit(conn);
        return true;
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace soft_delete_records
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Connection Handling**: The function connects to `test_db.sqlite` using `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()`.

2. **Transaction Management**: Uses `sqlgen::begin_transaction()` to start a transaction, ensuring atomicity.

3. **Dynamic Query Generation**: Constructs an SQL query with an `IN` clause that dynamically generates the required number of placeholders (`?`) based on the input IDs.

4. **Parameterized Execution**: Executes the query using `conn.execute()` with the IDs vector as parameters, preventing SQL injection.

5. **Error Handling**: Checks the execution result and rolls back the transaction if any error occurs. Returns `false` on failure or exception.

6. **Efficiency**: Uses a single SQL statement for batch updates, which is more efficient than individual updates.

The solution ensures data integrity through transactions and handles edge cases like empty input vectors gracefully.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace transaction_transfer_funds {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
};

bool transfer_funds(int from_account_id, int to_account_id, double amount) {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    try {
        // Begin transaction
        conn.execute("BEGIN TRANSACTION;");
        
        // Check and deduct from sender account
        auto result1 = conn.execute(
            "UPDATE Account SET balance = balance - ? WHERE account_id = ? AND balance >= ?;",
            amount, from_account_id, amount
        );
        
        if (result1.affected_rows() == 0) {
            conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
            return false; // Insufficient funds or account not found
        }
        
        // Add to receiver account
        auto result2 = conn.execute(
            "UPDATE Account SET balance = balance + ? WHERE account_id = ?;",
            amount, to_account_id
        );
        
        if (result2.affected_rows() == 0) {
            conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
            return false; // Receiver account not found
        }
        
        // Commit transaction
        conn.execute("COMMIT;");
        return true;
        
    } catch (...) {
        // Rollback on any exception
        conn.execute("ROLLBACK;");
        return false;
    }
}

}  // namespace transaction_transfer_funds
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"

2. **Transaction Management**: 
   - Starts with `BEGIN TRANSACTION;`
   - Commits with `COMMIT;` if both updates succeed
   - Rolls back with `ROLLBACK;` on any failure

3. **Fund Transfer Logic**:
   - First update deducts funds only if `balance >= amount` (atomic check-and-deduct)
   - Second update adds funds to receiver
   - Checks `affected_rows()` after each operation to verify success

4. **Error Handling**:
   - Catches all exceptions with `catch (...)` to ensure proper rollback
   - Returns `false` on any failure, `true` on successful transfer

This solution ensures atomicity, consistency, and proper error handling for the fund transfer operation.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
// eas_common.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

using namespace sqlgen::literals;

struct Student {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>> SNo;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> SName;
  int Sex = 1;
  std::optional<sqlgen::DateTime> Birthday;
  std::optional<uint32_t> Major;
  std::optional<double> Scores = 0.0;
  std::optional<std::string> Remark;
  std::optional<int> Age; // Added for the second task
};

struct Course {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> CID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<50> CName;
  std::optional<int> Term = 1;
  std::optional<int> CTime;
  int Credit = 0;
};

struct Score {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t, sqlgen::auto_incr> ScID;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<sqlgen::Varchar<10>, Student, "SNo"> SNo;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<uint32_t, Course, "CID"> CID;
  std::optional<double> Score;
  std::optional<char> Grade; // Added for the first task
};

struct Major {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<uint32_t> MID;
  sqlgen::Varchar<30> MName;
};

const std::string db_path = "data/eas_database.db";

void design_eas_database_without_major() {
  std::filesystem::create_directories("data");
  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then([](auto&& conn) {
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON;");
      })
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Student> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Course> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

void design_eas_database_with_major() {
  design_eas_database_without_major();

  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Major> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)
      .value();
}

// update_eas_table.hpp
#pragma once

#include <filesystem>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

#include "eas_common.hpp"

namespace update_eas_table {

void update_eas_table() {
  design_eas_database_with_major();
  
  sqlgen::sqlite::connect(db_path)
      .and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_exists)  // Drop Score to add Grade
      .and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Score> | sqlgen::if_not_exists)  // Recreate with Grade
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        // Add Age column to Student table
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "ALTER TABLE Student ADD COLUMN Age INTEGER NULL;");
      })
      .and_then([](auto conn) {
        // Drop Major table
        return sqlgen::exec(conn, "DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Major;");
      })
      .value();
}

} // namespace update_eas_table
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Add Grade Column:** The `Score` struct is modified to include a `Grade` field. The existing `Score` table is dropped and recreated to apply this change.
2. **Modify Age Column:** A new `Age` column is added to the `Student` table using raw SQL since direct column modification isn't supported by `sqlgen`.
3. **Drop Major Table:** The `Major` table is dropped using a raw SQL command.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <optional>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace upsert_user_profile {

struct UserProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::optional<std::string> bio;
  std::optional<std::string> avatar_url;
};

bool upsert_user_profile(const UserProfile& u) {
    try {
        // Connect to the SQLite database
        auto conn_result = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
        if (!conn_result) {
            return false;
        }
        
        auto conn = conn_result.value();
        
        // Execute the UPSERT operation using INSERT OR REPLACE
        auto result = sqlgen::write(conn, 
            "INSERT OR REPLACE INTO UserProfile (user_id, bio, avatar_url) VALUES (?, ?, ?)", 
            u.user_id, u.bio, u.avatar_url);
            
        return result.has_value();
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        // Handle any exceptions (e.g., database errors)
        return false;
    }
}

} // namespace upsert_user_profile
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite"
2. **UPSERT Operation**: Executes `INSERT OR REPLACE` which handles both insert and update scenarios:
   - If `user_id` exists: Updates the existing record
   - If `user_id` doesn't exist: Inserts a new record
3. **Parameter Handling**: Uses `std::optional` which automatically maps to `NULL` in SQL for missing values
4. **Error Handling**: Wraps the operation in a try-catch block and returns `false` on any exception
5. **Return Value**: Returns `true` if the operation succeeds, `false` otherwise

This solution leverages SQLite's built-in `INSERT OR REPLACE` functionality for efficient upsert operations while maintaining clean error handling.</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <ranges>
#include <rfl.hpp>
#include <rfl/json.hpp>
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int stock;
};

struct Sale {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int, sqlgen::auto_incr> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  double amount;
};

struct SalesSummary {
  static constexpr bool is_view = true;
  std::string product_name;
  int total_quantity;
  double total_revenue;
};

/**
 * Function: Create a view SalesSummary that aggregates sales by product.
 * 
 * @return true if view creation succeeds, false otherwise
 */
bool create_view_sales_summary() {
  try {
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Build the query to join Sale and Product tables and aggregate sales
    auto query = sqlgen::from_<Sale, "s">() |
                 sqlgen::join<Product, "p">("product_id"_s == "product_id"_p) |
                 sqlgen::select("p.name"_p | as<"product_name">,
                               "sum(s.quantity)"_s | as<"total_quantity">,
                               "sum(s.amount)"_s | as<"total_revenue">) |
                 sqlgen::group_by("p.name"_p);
    
    // Create the view using the SalesSummary struct for type validation
    auto result = conn.create_view<SalesSummary>("SalesSummary", query);
    
    return result.has_value();
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace schema_create_view_sales_summary
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to establish a connection to `test_db.sqlite`.

2. **Query Construction**: 
   - Starts with the `Sale` table aliased as `s`
   - Joins with `Product` table aliased as `p` on `product_id`
   - Selects `product_name` from `Product`, and aggregates `quantity` and `amount` from `Sale`
   - Groups results by `product_name` to get per-product summaries

3. **View Creation**: Uses `create_view<SalesSummary>` to create the view, leveraging the `SalesSummary` struct for type safety and column validation.

4. **Error Handling**: Wraps the entire operation in a try-catch block to return `false` on any exception, ensuring the function doesn't crash.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>

namespace right_join_departments_projects {

struct Department {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Project {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> project_id;
  int department_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct DepProj {
  std::optional<std::string> department_name;
  std::string project_name;
};

std::vector<DepProj> right_join_departments_projects() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to SQLite database
  auto conn = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Execute RIGHT JOIN query
  auto result = conn.and_then(
    sqlgen::read<std::vector<DepProj>> |
    from<Project>() |
    right_join<Department>([](const Project& p, const Department& d) {
      return p.department_id == d.department_id;
    }) |
    select([](const Project& p, const Department& d) {
      return DepProj{
        d.name,  // department name (optional)
        p.name   // project name
      };
    })
  );

  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace right_join_departments_projects
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to connect to the SQLite database
2. **RIGHT JOIN Logic**: Performs `Project RIGHT JOIN Department` to include all departments
3. **Join Condition**: Matches projects with their respective departments using `department_id`
4. **Result Mapping**: Maps the joined results to `DepProj` structs with department names as optional and project names as required
5. **Error Handling**: Uses `and_then()` to handle potential database errors and `result.value()` to extract the final data

The solution correctly implements the right join requirement while handling the optional nature of department names through the `std::optional` type.</answer>

};

                      .where("B.score > 10 AND A.name LIKE '%x%'")
                      .order_by("B.score", sqlgen::OrderBy::DESC)
                      .limit(5);

}  // namespace generate_sql_dry_run

<answer>```cpp
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <string>

namespace analyze_temporal_data {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> start_time;
  std::optional<sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S">> end_time;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

struct TemporalAnalysis {
  int event_id;
  std::string event_name;
  int start_day;
  int start_month;
  int start_year;
  int start_hour;
  int start_minute;
  int start_second;
  int start_weekday;
  int64_t start_epoch;
  std::optional<double> duration_days;
};

std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyze_temporal_data(const std::vector<Event>& events) {
  try {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create a temporary table to hold the events
    conn.and_then(sqlgen::sqlite::execute(
        "CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE events_temp ("
        "event_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, "
        "event_name TEXT NOT NULL, "
        "start_time TEXT NOT NULL, "
        "end_time TEXT, "
        "created_at TEXT NOT NULL)"
    ));
    
    // Insert all events into the temporary table
    for (const auto& event : events) {
      conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(event).into("events_temp"));
    }
    
    // Execute the query to extract temporal data
    auto query_result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::sqlite::execute(
        "SELECT "
        "event_id, "
        "event_name, "
        "CAST(strftime('%d', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%m', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%Y', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%H', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%M', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%S', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%w', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "CAST(strftime('%%s', start_time) AS INTEGER), "
        "(julianday(end_time) - julianday(start_time)) "
        "FROM events_temp "
        "WHERE end_time IS NOT NULL"
    ));
    
    if (!query_result) {
      return {};
    }
    
    // Process the query results
    std::vector<TemporalAnalysis> analyses;
    auto& rows = *query_result;
    
    for (size_t i = 0; i < rows.size(); ++i) {
      const auto& row = rows[i];
      
      TemporalAnalysis analysis;
      analysis.event_id = row.get<int>(0);
      analysis.event_name = row.get<std::string>(1);
      analysis.start_day = row.get<int>(2);
      analysis.start_month = row.get<int>(3);
      analysis.start_year = row.get<int>(4);
      analysis.start_hour = row.get<int>(5);
      analysis.start_minute = row.get<int>(6);
      analysis.start_second = row.get<int>(7);
      analysis.start_weekday = row.get<int>(8);
      analysis.start_epoch = row.get<int64_t>(9);
      
      // Calculate duration days if end_time exists
      if (row.column_count() > 10 && !row.is_null(10)) {
        analysis.duration_days = row.get<double>(10);
      }
      
      analyses.push_back(std::move(analysis));
    }
    
    return analyses;
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Return empty vector on any error
    return {};
  }
}

} // namespace analyze_temporal_data
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database file "test_db.sqlite"
2. Creates a temporary table to store the input events
3. Inserts all events into the temporary table
4. Executes a comprehensive SQL query using SQLite's date/time functions
5. Processes the query results into the required TemporalAnalysis structure
6. Handles errors gracefully by returning an empty vector
7. Uses proper type casting for SQLite's string-based date functions
8. Calculates duration using Julian day arithmetic for precise day differences

The solution demonstrates proper use of SQLite's date/time functionality while maintaining type safety and error handling.</answer>

bool upsert_inventory_items(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {

  std::string name;
  int quantity;
};

bool upsert_inventory_items(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {
  try {
    // Connect to the SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Begin a transaction to ensure atomic operations
    auto session = conn.begin_transaction();
    
    // Perform the upsert operation using sqlgen's write API

    session.write(items);
    
    // Commit the transaction to save changes
    session.commit();
    
    // Read back all items from the Inventory table

    if (!result.has_value()) {

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace limit_recent_events {

struct Event {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> event_id;
  std::string name;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> created_at;
};

std::vector<Event> limit_recent_events(size_t n = 10) {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<Event>> | 
                sqlgen::order_by("created_at", sqlgen::desc) | 
                sqlgen::limit(n))
      .value();
}
}  // namespace limit_recent_events
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.
2. **Query Execution**: 
   - `sqlgen::read<std::vector<Event>>` reads all events from the database.
   - `| sqlgen::order_by("created_at", sqlgen::desc)` sorts events by creation time in descending order (most recent first).
   - `| sqlgen::limit(n)` restricts the result to the `n` most recent events.
3. **Result Extraction**: `.value()` extracts the final result from the monadic chain, returning a `std::vector<Event>` containing the limited recent events.</answer>

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <optional>

namespace analyze_social_network {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::string email;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<> join_date;
  std::string city;
};

struct Friendship {
  int user1_id;
  int user2_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<> created_at;
};

struct FriendConnection {
  std::string user1_name;
  std::string user2_name;
};

std::vector<FriendConnection> analyze_social_network_impl(const std::vector<User>& users, const std::vector<Friendship>& friendships) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Drop tables if they exist
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<User> | sqlgen::if_exists);
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Friendship> | sqlgen::if_exists);
  
  // Create tables
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::create_table<User>);
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Friendship>);
  
  // Insert data
  for (const auto& user : users) {
    conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(user));
  }
  for (const auto& friendship : friendships) {
    conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(friendship));
  }
  
  // Execute the mutual friends query
  auto query = R"(
    SELECT u1.username, u2.username, COUNT(f1.user_id2) as mutual_friends
    FROM User u1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
    INNER JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id2 = u2.user_id
    WHERE u1.user_id < u2.user_id
      AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f3 
        WHERE f3.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f3.user_id2 = u2.user_id
      )
    GROUP BY u1.user_id, u2.user_id
    HAVING COUNT(f1.user_id2) > 0
  )";
  
  auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::raw_query(query))
    .transform([](auto&& rows) {
      std::vector<FriendConnection> connections;
      for (const auto& row : rows) {
        connections.push_back({
          row.get<std::string>("username"), // u1.username
          row.get<std::string>("username")  // u2.username - but need to distinguish
        });
      }
      return connections;
    });
  
  // Wait, the query returns u1.username and u2.username, but in the result, they are both labeled as "username". Need to alias them properly.
  // So the query should use AS to give distinct column names.
  
  // Corrected query with aliases:
  auto corrected_query = R"(
    SELECT u1.username as user1, u2.username as user2, COUNT(f1.user_id2) as mutual_friends
    FROM User u1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
    INNER JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id2 = u2.user_id
    WHERE u1.user_id < u2.user_id
      AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f3 
        WHERE f3.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f3.user_id2 = u2.user_id
      )
    GROUP BY u1.user_id, u2.user_id
    HAVING COUNT(f1.user_id2) > 0
  )";
  
  // Then in the transform, get "user1" and "user2" columns.
  
  auto final_result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::raw_query(corrected_query))
    .transform([](auto&& rows) {
      std::vector<FriendConnection> connections;
      for (const auto& row : rows) {
        connections.push_back({
          row.get<std::string>("user1"),
          row.get<std::string>("user2")
        });
      }
      return connections;
    });
  
  return final_result.value_or(std::vector<FriendConnection>{});
}

}  // namespace analyze_social_network

// The main function would call this implementation
std::vector<analyze_social_network::FriendConnection> analyze_social_network() {
  // Example usage with sample data
  std::vector<analyze_social_network::User> users = {
    {1, "alice", "alice@example.com", "2023-01-01 10:00:00", "New York"},
    {2, "bob", "bob@example.com", "2023-01-02 11:00:00", "New York"},
    {3, "charlie", "charlie@example.com", "2023-01-03 12:00:00", "London"},
    {4, "diana", "diana@example.com", "2023-01-04 13:00:00", "New York"}
  };
  
  std::vector<analyze_social_network::Friendship> friendships = {
    {1, 2, "2023-01-01 10:30:00"},  // alice and bob
    {2, 3, "2023-01-02 11:30:00"},  // bob and charlie
    {1, 3, "2023-01-03 12:30:00"},  // alice and charlie
    {3, 4, "2023-01-04 13:30:00"}   // charlie and diana
  };
  
  return analyze_social_network_impl(users, friendships);
}
```

This code connects to the SQLite database, creates the necessary tables, inserts the provided data, runs the mutual friends query using a self-join and nested EXISTS clause, and returns the results as FriendConnection objects. The query finds pairs of users who are mutual friends (i.e., they have at least one mutual friend and are themselves friends).

<answer>```cpp
#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <optional>

namespace analyze_social_network {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string username;
  std::string email;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<> join_date;
  std::string city;
};

struct Friendship {
  int user1_id;
  int user2_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<> created_at;
};

struct FriendConnection {
  std::string user1_name;
  std::string user2_name;
};

std::vector<FriendConnection> analyze_social_network_impl(const std::vector<User>& users, const std::vector<Friendship>& friendships) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  
  // Ensure tables are dropped and recreated
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<User> | sqlgen::if_exists);
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::drop_table<Friendship> | sqlgen::if_exists);
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::create_table<User>);
  conn.and_then(sqlgen::create_table<Friendship>);
  
  // Insert data into tables
  for (const auto& user : users) {
    conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(user));
  }
  for (const auto& friendship : friendships) {
    conn.and_then(sqlgen::insert(friendship));
  }
  
  // Execute the mutual friends query using raw SQL
  const std::string query = R"(
    SELECT u1.username as user1, u2.username as user2, COUNT(f1.user_id2) as mutual_count
    FROM User u1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f1 ON u1.user_id = f1.user_id1
    INNER JOIN Friendship f2 ON f1.user_id2 = f2.user_id1
    INNER JOIN User u2 ON f2.user_id2 = u2.user_id
    WHERE u1.user_id < u2.user_id
      AND EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM Friendship f3 
        WHERE f3.user_id1 = u1.user_id AND f3.user_id2 = u2.user_id
      )
    GROUP BY u1.user_id, u2.user_id
    HAVING mutual_count > 0
  )";
  
  auto result = conn.and_then(sqlgen::raw_query(query))
    .transform([](auto&& rows) {
      std::vector<FriendConnection> connections;
      for (const auto& row : rows) {
        connections.push_back({
          row.get<std::string>("user1"),
          row.get<std::string>("user2")
        });
      }
      return connections;
    });
  
  return result.value_or(std::vector<FriendConnection>{});
}

}  // namespace analyze_social_network

// Main function to demonstrate usage

  const auto users = std::vector<analyze_social_network::User>({

    {1, 3, "2023-01-03 12:30:00"},
    {3, 4, "2023-01-04 13:30:00"}
  });

  return analyze_social_network_impl(users, friendships);
}

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace mathematical_operations_analysis {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string name;
  double price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
};

struct MathAnalysis {
  int product_id;
  std::string name;
  double abs_price;
  double ceil_price;
  double floor_price;
  double rounded_price;
  double exp_discount;
  double ln_price;
  double log2_quantity;
  double sin_discount;
  double cos_discount;
  double tan_discount;
  double sqrt_quantity;
};

std::vector<MathAnalysis> analyze_mathematical_operations(const std::vector<Product>& products) {
  auto conn = sqlgen::sqlite::connect();
  
  // Create the Product table and insert data
  conn.create_table<Product>().execute();
  sqlgen::write(conn, products).value();
  
  // Execute the mathematical analysis query
  auto result = conn.and_then(
      sqlgen::read<std::vector<MathAnalysis>>(
          R"(SELECT product_id, name, ABS(price) as abs_price, CEIL(price) as ceil_price, FLOOR(price) as floor_price, ROUND(price, 2) as rounded_price, EXP(discount_rate/100) as exp_discount, LN(price) as ln_price, LOG2(quantity) as log2_quantity, SIN(discount_rate) as sin_discount, COS(discount_rate) as cos_discount, TAN(discount_rate) as tan_discount, SQRT(quantity) as sqrt_quantity FROM Product)"
      )
  ).value();
  
  return result;
}

}  // namespace mathematical_operations_analysis
```

This solution uses the `sqlgen` library to:
1. Connect to an SQLite database
2. Create a temporary table from the `Product` struct
3. Insert all products into the table
4. Execute a comprehensive SQL query with mathematical operations
5. Return the results mapped to the `MathAnalysis` struct

The query performs all required mathematical operations including trigonometric, logarithmic, exponential, and rounding functions on the product data.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace trim_and_length_validation {

struct UserProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string name;
  std::string email;
};

struct ValidatedProfile {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> user_id;
  std::string trimmed_name;
  std::string trimmed_email;
  int name_length;
  int email_length;
};

std::vector<ValidatedProfile> trim_and_length_validation() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<ValidatedProfile>>[
          "SELECT user_id, TRIM(name) as trimmed_name, TRIM(email) as trimmed_email, "
          "LENGTH(TRIM(name)) as name_length, LENGTH(TRIM(email)) as email_length "
          "FROM UserProfile;"
      ])
      .value();
}

}  // namespace trim_and_length_validation
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>

namespace calculate_order_metrics {

struct OrderItem {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  int order_id;
  int product_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  std::optional<double> discount_rate;
  std::optional<double> tax_rate;
};

struct OrderMetrics {
  int item_id;
  int order_id;
  int product_id;
  double unit_price;
  int quantity;
  double discount_rate;
  double tax_rate;
  double gross_total;
  double discounted_total;
  double final_total;
  double unit_cost;
  int quantity_group;
  double estimated_profit;
};

std::vector<OrderMetrics> calculate_order_metrics_impl(const std::vector<OrderItem>& items) {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    try {
        // Connect to an in-memory SQLite database
        auto conn = sqlite::connect(":memory:")
            .and_then(create_table<OrderItem>)
            .and_then(insert(items))
            .and_then(sqlgen::sql(
                R"(SELECT "
                    item_id, "
                    order_id, "
                    product_id, "
                    unit_price, "
                    quantity, "
                    COALESCE(discount_rate, 0) as discount_rate, "
                    COALESCE(tax_rate, 0) as tax_rate, "
                    (unit_price * quantity) as gross_total, "
                    (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100)) as discounted_total, "
                    (unit_price * quantity * (1 - COALESCE(discount_rate, 0)/100) * (1 + COALESCE(tax_rate, 0)/100)) as final_total, "
                    (unit_price * quantity / quantity) as unit_cost, "
                    (quantity % 10) as quantity_group, "
                    (unit_price * 0.3) as estimated_profit "
                FROM OrderItem)"
            ))
            .and_then(sqlgen::read<std::vector<OrderMetrics>>);

        if (!conn) {
            throw std::runtime_error("Database operation failed");
        }

        return conn.value();
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        throw std::runtime_error(std::string("Failed to calculate order metrics: ") + e.what());
    }
}

}  // namespace calculate_order_metrics
```

This solution:
1. Creates an in-memory SQLite database connection
2. Creates the OrderItem table schema automatically from the struct
3. Inserts all provided OrderItem records
4. Executes a SQL query with all required arithmetic operations
5. Maps the result set to OrderMetrics structs
6. Handles optional discount_rate and tax_rate with COALESCE
7. Includes proper error handling for database operations
8. Follows the exact calculations specified in the reference SQL</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace join_orders_customers_payments {

struct Customer {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> customer_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Order {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  int customer_id;
  sqlgen::Timestamp<"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"> order_date;
};

struct Payment {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> payment_id;
  int order_id;
  double amount;
};

struct Consolidated {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> order_id;
  std::string customer_name;
  double amount;
};

std::vector<Consolidated> join_orders_customers_payments() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then([](auto&& db) {
        return db.read<Order>()
            .inner_join<Customer>([](const auto& o, const auto& c) {
              return o.customer_id == c.customer_id;
            })
            .inner_join<Payment>([](const auto& o, const auto& p) {
              return o.order_id == p.order_id;
            })
            .order_by([](const auto& row) { return row.amount; }, sqlgen::order::desc)
            .transform([](const auto& row) {
              return Consolidated{
                  row.order_id,
                  row.name,
                  row.amount
              };
            });
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace join_orders_customers_payments
```

This solution:
1. Connects to the SQLite database
2. Performs inner joins between Order-Customer and Order-Payment tables
3. Selects the required columns (order_id, customer_name, amount)
4. Orders results by payment amount in descending order
5. Returns a vector of Consolidated structs containing the joined data</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace replace_text_in_notes {

struct Note {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string content;
};

struct UpdatedNote {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> note_id;
  std::string updated_content;
};

std::vector<UpdatedNote> replace_text_in_notes() {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;

  // Connect to the SQLite database and perform operations
  auto result = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(update<Note> | set("content"_c, "REPLACE(content, 'foo', 'bar')"))
      .and_then(select_from<Note> | where(true))
      .transform([](const auto& notes) {
        std::vector<UpdatedNote> updated_notes;
        for (const auto& note : notes) {
          updated_notes.push_back(UpdatedNote{
            note.note_id,
            note.content  // Content now contains replaced text
          });
        }
        return updated_notes;
      })
      .value();

  return result;
}

}  // namespace replace_text_in_notes
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")` to establish a connection to the SQLite database.

2. **Text Replacement**: The `update<Note>` operation uses the SQL `REPLACE` function to substitute all occurrences of "foo" with "bar" in the `content` column.

3. **Data Retrieval**: After the update, `select_from<Note>` retrieves all notes from the database.

4. **Result Transformation**: The `transform` method converts the retrieved `Note` objects into `UpdatedNote` objects containing the modified content.

5. **Error Handling**: The `.value()` at the end assumes successful operations (as per problem constraints).

This solution efficiently performs the required string replacement and returns the updated notes using the sqlgen library's fluent interface.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read {

struct Inventory {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> item_id;
  std::string name;
  int quantity;
  std::string location;
};

bool inventory_bulk_write_and_read(const std::vector<Inventory>& items) {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
      .and_then(drop<Inventory> | if_exists)
      .and_then(create_as<Inventory> | or_replace)
      .and_then(write(items))
      .and_then(
          select_from<Inventory> | where("quantity > 50") | order_by("quantity DESC")
      )
      .transform([](const auto& result) {
        return !result.empty();
      })
      .value();
}

}  // namespace inventory_bulk_write_and_read
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>

namespace cascade_delete_demo {

struct Parent {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> parent_id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Child {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> child_id;
  sqlgen::ForeignKey<int, Parent, "parent_id"> parent_id;
  std::string note;
};

bool cascade_delete_demo() {
  try {
    auto result = sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Create Parent table
          return conn.create_table<Parent>();
        })
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Create Child table with foreign key constraint
          return conn.create_table<Child>();
        })
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Insert a parent record
          Parent parent{1, "Test Parent"};
          return conn.insert(parent);
        })
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Insert a child record linked to the parent
          Child child{1, 1, "Test Note"};
          return conn.insert(child);
        })
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Delete the parent record
          return conn.delete_from<Parent>().where("parent_id = 1").execute();
        })
        .and_then([](auto conn) {
          // Verify if child record was automatically deleted
          auto child_count = conn.select_from<Child>().where("parent_id = 1").count();
          return child_count == 0;
        })
        .value();
    
    return result;
  } catch (...) {
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace cascade_delete_demo
```

This solution demonstrates the cascade delete functionality by:
1. Creating two related tables with proper foreign key constraints
2. Inserting test data with parent-child relationships
3. Deleting a parent record and automatically removing associated child records
4. Returning a boolean result indicating whether the cascade delete operation succeeded</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

namespace update_set_operations {

struct Account {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> account_id;
  double balance;
  std::string status;
};

bool update_set_operations() {
  try {
    // Connect to SQLite database
    auto conn = sqlgen::connect("sqlite:test_db.sqlite");
    
    // Create Account table
    sqlgen::write::create_table<Account>(conn);
    
    // Insert initial data
    sqlgen::write::insert(conn, Account{1, 100.0, "active"});
    sqlgen::write::insert(conn, Account{2, 200.0, "inactive"});
    
    // Update account 1: set balance to 150.0 and status to "pending"
    sqlgen::write::update(conn, Account{})
        .where(Account::account_id == 1)
        .set(Account::balance = 150.0, Account::status = "pending")
        .execute();
    
    // Update account 2: add 50.0 to balance and set status to "active"
    sqlgen::write::update(conn, Account{})
        .where(Account::account_id == 2)
        .set(Account::balance = Account::balance + 50.0, Account::status = "active")
        .execute();
    
    // Verify changes by reading all accounts
    auto accounts = sqlgen::read::all<Account>(conn);
    
    // Check if exactly 2 accounts exist
    if (accounts.size() != 2) {
      return false;
    }
    
    // Verify account 1
    auto acc1 = std::find_if(accounts.begin(), accounts.end(),
        [](const Account& a) { return a.account_id == 1; });
    if (acc1 == accounts.end() || 
        acc1->balance != 150.0 || 
        acc1->status != "pending") {
      return false;
    }
    
    // Verify account 2  
    auto acc2 = std::find_if(accounts.begin(), accounts.end(),
        [](const Account& a) { return a.account_id == 2; });
    if (acc2 == accounts.end() ||
        acc2->balance != 250.0 || 
        acc2->status != "active") {
      return false;
    }
    
    return true;
    
  } catch (const std::exception& e) {
    // Return false on any error
    return false;
  }
}

}  // namespace update_set_operations
```

This solution:
1. Connects to SQLite database "test_db.sqlite"
2. Creates the Account table with proper schema
3. Inserts initial test data
4. Performs two update operations using different SET patterns:
   - Direct value assignment
   - Expression-based assignment (balance + 50.0)
5. Verifies the updates persisted by reading and validating the data
6. Returns true if all operations succeed, false otherwise</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>

namespace select_from_read_to_vector {

struct Book {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> book_id;
  std::string title;
  int pages;
};

std::vector<Book> select_from_read_to_vector() {
  return sqlgen::sqlite::connect()
      .and_then(sqlgen::select_from<Book> | sqlgen::order_by("pages", sqlgen::desc) | sqlgen::to<std::vector<Book>>)
      .value();
}

} // namespace select_from_read_to_vector
```

**Explanation:**
1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect()` to establish a connection to an SQLite database
2. **Query Execution**: Executes `SELECT * FROM books ORDER BY pages DESC` using the type-safe `select_from<Book>` API
3. **Result Conversion**: Converts the query results to a `std::vector<Book>` using `to<std::vector<Book>>`
4. **Error Handling**: The `.value()` call extracts the result, assuming successful execution (exception would be thrown on failure)
5. **Return Value**: Returns books sorted by page count in descending order as required</answer>

- Structure the results into JoinResult objects, which can have optional fields since some joins might leave some fields null.

Let me think about each join:

INNER JOIN: Only matches where department_id exists in both tables. So for each employee with a valid department_id that exists in departments, we get their info plus department name.

LEFT JOIN: All employees, even if their department_id doesn't exist. So the department_name would be nullopt in such cases.

RIGHT JOIN: All departments, even if they have no employees. So employees would be nullopt here.

FULL JOIN: All employees and all departments. This requires combining LEFT and RIGHT joins, excluding duplicates. But since SQLite doesn't support FULL JOIN directly, we can do a UNION of LEFT and RIGHT joins, then remove duplicates. Alternatively, use a UNION ALL and handle duplicates in the result. But for simplicity, maybe the code will use a UNION approach.

Wait, but how to handle the RIGHT JOIN in SQLite? Because SQLite doesn't have RIGHT JOIN syntax. Oh right, RIGHT JOIN is equivalent to LEFT JOIN with reversed table order. So to get a RIGHT JOIN, we can swap the tables and do a LEFT JOIN. For example:

SELECT d.*, e.* FROM Employee e LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

But that's actually a LEFT JOIN with tables reversed, which gives us all departments (right table) and matching employees. Wait, no. Wait, in a RIGHT JOIN, the right table (Department) is the one that's fully included. So to simulate a RIGHT JOIN in SQLite, you can reverse the tables and do a LEFT JOIN. So:

SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
FROM Department d
LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id;

This would give all departments (right table in original RIGHT JOIN), and employees where the department_id matches. So this is equivalent to a RIGHT JOIN.

Similarly, for FULL JOIN, we can combine the results of LEFT JOIN and the simulated RIGHT JOIN, then remove duplicates. So:

SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
FROM Employee e
LEFT JOIN Department d ON e.department_id = d.department_id

UNION

SELECT d.department_name, e.first_name, e.last_name, e.salary
FROM Department d
LEFT JOIN Employee e ON d.department_id = e.department_id;

This would give all employees (left side) plus all departments (right side), effectively a FULL JOIN.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace concat_function {

struct Person {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string first_name;
  std::string last_name;
};

struct FullName {
  std::string full_name;
};

inline auto concat_function() {
    return sqlgen::select_from<Person>()
        | sqlgen::transform([](const auto& person) {
            return FullName{
                .full_name = sqlgen::concat(
                    sqlgen::column("first_name"),
                    " ",
                    sqlgen::column("last_name")
                )
            };
        });
}

}  // namespace concat_function
```

**Explanation:**

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

namespace in_operator_usage {

struct Status {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int code;
};

 * Returns: std::string.
 */
inline std::string in_operator_usage() {
  auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Status>().where("code", sqlgen::in({1, 2, 3}));
  return query.to_sql();
}

}  // namespace in_operator_usage
```

5. **SQL Generation:** Converts the constructed query to a SQL string using `to_sql()` and returns it.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <vector>
#include <string>

namespace get_top_products {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double price;
};

struct Sales {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> sale_id;
  int product_id;
  int quantity;
  sqlgen::Date sale_date;
};

struct TopProduct {
  int product_id;
  std::string product_name;
  std::string category;
  double total_sales;
};

std::vector<TopProduct> get_top_products(size_t limit_count = 10) {
    using namespace sqlgen;
    using namespace sqlgen::literals;

    return sqlgen::sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite")
        .and_then([limit_count](auto conn) -> sqlgen::Result<std::vector<TopProduct>> {
            auto query = select_from<Product>()
                .join<Sales>("product_id"_f == "product_id"_f)
                .select({
                    "product_id"_f,
                    "product_name"_f,
                    "category"_f,
                    sum("price"_f * "quantity"_f).as("total_sales"_f)
                })
                .group_by("product_id"_f, "product_name"_f, "category"_f)
                .order_by("total_sales"_f, sqlgen::order::desc)
                .limit(limit_count);

            return conn.and_then([&](auto c) {
                return c.execute(query).transform([](auto&& res) {
                    return res.value();
                });
            });
        })
        .value();
}

}  // namespace get_top_products
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Database Connection**: Uses `sqlgen::sqlite::connect` to establish a connection to "test_db.sqlite".

2. **Query Construction**: Builds a SQL query using sqlgen's fluent DSL:
   - **Join**: Combines `Product` and `Sales` tables on `product_id`
   - **Aggregation**: Calculates `SUM(price * quantity)` as `total_sales`
   - **Grouping**: Groups results by product attributes
   - **Ordering**: Sorts by `total_sales` in descending order
   - **Limit**: Restricts results to the specified count

3. **Execution**: Executes the query and transforms the result into a `std::vector<TopProduct>`.

4. **Error Handling**: Uses `Result<T>` monad for proper error propagation through the `and_then` chain.

The solution efficiently retrieves top-selling products while maintaining type safety and leveraging sqlgen's expressive query DSL.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace order_by_descending {

struct Score {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int points;
};

/**

 */
inline std::string order_by_descending() {
    auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Score>().order_by(&Score::points, sqlgen::order::desc);
    return sqlgen::sqlite::to_sql(query);
}

}  // namespace order_by_descending
```

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <unordered_map>

namespace update_inventory_stock {

struct Product {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> product_id;
  int stock;
};

bool update_inventory_stock(const std::unordered_map<int, int>& received) {
  using namespace sqlgen;
  using namespace sqlgen::literals;
  
  // Connect to database and create table if needed
  auto conn_res = sqlite::connect("test_db.sqlite");
  if (!conn_res) return false;
  create_table<Product>(conn_res.value());

  // Start transaction
  auto txn_res = begin_transaction(conn_res.value());
  if (!txn_res) return false;
  auto txn = txn_res.value();
  
  // Update stock for each product in the shipment
  for (const auto& [product_id, quantity] : received) {
    std::string sql = "UPDATE Product SET stock = stock + ? WHERE product_id = ?;";
    auto exec_res = exec(txn, sql, quantity, product_id);
    if (!exec_res) {
      // Rollback transaction on failure
      rollback(txn);
      return false;
    }
  }
  
  // Commit transaction if all updates succeeded
  auto commit_res = commit(txn);
  return commit_res.has_value();
}

}  // namespace update_inventory_stock
```

**Key Improvements:**
1. **Parameterized Queries**: Uses `?` placeholders and passes parameters separately to prevent SQL injection
2. **Transaction Safety**: Explicitly rolls back transaction if any update fails
3. **Proper Resource Management**: Ensures transaction is properly committed or rolled back
4. **Type Safety**: Leverages sqlgen's type handling for database operations

The solution maintains atomicity by wrapping all updates in a single transaction and ensures data integrity through proper error handling.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>
#include <sqlgen/sqlite.hpp>
#include <string>

namespace left_join_explicit {

struct User {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string name;
};

struct Post {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  int user_id;
  std::string title;
};

struct Product {
    sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
    std::string name;
};

inline std::string like_pattern_matching() {
    auto query = sqlgen::select_from<Product> | 
        where([](const Product& p) { 
            return p.name.like("A%"); 
        });
    return query.to_sql();
}

inline std::string left_join_explicit() {
  const auto users = std::vector<User>{
      {0, "Homer"},
      {1, "Marge"},
      {2, "Bart"},
      {3, "Lisa"},
      {4, "Maggie"}
  };

  const auto posts = std::vector<Post>{
      {0, 0, "Homer's post"},
      {1, 1, "Marge's post"},
      {2, 2, "Bart's post"}
  };

  const auto db = sqlgen::sqlite::connect();
  db.and_then(sqlgen::write(std::ref(users)));
  db.and_then(sqlgen::write(std::ref(posts)));

  const auto get_name = [](const auto& obj) { return obj.name; };
  const auto get_title = [](const auto& obj) { return obj.title; };

  auto result = db.and_then(
      sqlgen::left_join<User, Post, int>(
          sqlgen::on(User::id == Post::user_id),
          sqlgen::select_from<User, Post>(get_name, get_title)
      )
  );

  return result.value();
}

}  // namespace left_join_explicit
```

The solution defines a `Product` struct and constructs a query using `sqlgen` to select products where the `name` field matches the pattern `'A%'`. The `like_pattern_matching` function returns the generated SQL string. The `left_join_explicit` function remains unchanged from the original snippet.</answer>

#pragma once

#include <sqlgen.hpp>

#include <string>

namespace drop_table_if_exists {

struct Log {
  sqlgen::PrimaryKey<int> id;
  std::string message;
};

    return sqlgen::drop<Log>().if_exists().to_sql();
}

}  // namespace drop_table_if_exists
```

2. **Query Construction**: Uses `sqlgen::drop<Log>()` to create a DROP TABLE query for the `Log` table.
3. **Conditional Drop**: Chains `.if_exists()` to ensure the table is only dropped if it exists.
4. **SQL Generation**: Converts the query to a SQL string with `.to_sql()` and returns it.</answer>
